title: Singapore Noodles

overview.description: A Hong Kong-style stir-fried rice vermicelli dish with prawns, egg, vegetables and aromatic curry powder.

method: Soak the rice vermicelli according to the packet instructions until just tender.
Drain thoroughly and set aside.
Beat the eggs.
Heat 1 tsp of the oil in a wok or large frying pan.
Add the eggs and cook as a thin omelette.
Remove, allow to cool slightly and slice into thin strips.
Add another 1 tbsp of oil to the wok.
Add the prawns and stir-fry until just cooked through.
Remove and set aside.
Add the remaining oil.
Add the onion, red pepper, carrot and cabbage.
Stir-fry until the vegetables are just tender.
Add the garlic and cook for 30 seconds.
Add the curry powder and stir-fry briefly until fragrant.
Add the drained rice vermicelli.
Add the light soy sauce, sesame oil and white pepper.
Toss everything together over high heat until evenly combined and hot.
Return the prawns and sliced omelette to the wok.
Add the ham and spring onions.
Toss briefly to combine.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Serve immediately.

learning.did-you-know: Singapore noodles are generally considered a Hong Kong–style Cantonese dish rather than a traditional Singaporean dish. The name is believed to reflect the use of curry powder and Southeast Asian-inspired flavours.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare delicate rice vermicelli for stir-frying and how curry powder can be used as an aromatic seasoning rather than simply as the base of a curry.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Hong Kong-style noodle dish that adds thin rice vermicelli and curry powder to the collection, giving the children another completely different noodle texture and flavour profile.
chapman-tips: Don't over-soak or overcook the vermicelli; it should remain slightly firm before stir-frying.
Drain the noodles very thoroughly so they don't make the wok watery.
Prepare all the ingredients before starting to cook.
Use a mild curry powder for the main dish and add chilli separately if desired.
Add the prawns towards the end so they don't become tough.
The ham is traditional in many Hong Kong-style versions, but can be omitted or replaced with cooked chicken.
Keep the wok hot and toss the noodles gently to avoid breaking them.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The rice vermicelli can become soft and clump together when frozen and reheated.
notes.leftovers: Reheat quickly in a hot wok or frying pan. Leftovers can be eaten cold or at room temperature as part of a packed lunch.
